old_claim.note: Fine Arts (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the illuminated-manuscript node is anchored to early Insular and monastic manuscript production around 650-700, while fine_arts_medieval is now scoped to later Romanesque and Gothic fine arts from about 1000 onward.

source_shape.source_locator: The Met anchors Romanesque art to artistic activity in the eleventh and twelfth centuries, including monumental sculpture, frescoes, precious objects, and illuminated books.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source-backed fine_arts_medieval scope is later Romanesque and Gothic art, not early Insular manuscript illumination around 700.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes the direct edge because a later Romanesque/Gothic art complex cannot be a prerequisite for a node anchored to earlier codex and monastic manuscript production.

ontology_before: Illuminated Manuscripts & Scribal Art depended directly on broad Fine Arts (Medieval), which hid a chronology conflict after the fine-arts node was narrowed.
ontology_after: Illuminated Manuscripts & Scribal Art depends on codex production, pigments, writing, alchemy, and scriptoria without a direct Romanesque/Gothic fine-arts prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: illuminated_manuscripts_scribal_art no longer directly depends on fine_arts_medieval.
would_reject_if: fine_arts_medieval were broadened back to all early medieval visual art.
illuminated_manuscripts_scribal_art were rescoped to Romanesque and Gothic illuminated manuscripts after 1000.
A source showed the Book of Durrow or comparable early Insular manuscripts depended on later Romanesque/Gothic workshop practices.
short_reason: Early illuminated manuscripts predate the corrected Romanesque/Gothic fine-arts node.
